items.title: Risk scoring
items.description: Risk scoring in market intelligence summarises the estimated uncertainty or downside associated with an instrument or scenario into a comparable measure. It typically blends volatility, liquidity, concentration, and data-quality factors into an explainable score. The score is an analytical characterisation to aid comparison, not a directive to act. Systems should expose the contributing factors so users can see why something scores as it does rather than trusting an opaque number.
items.applicability: Applies to analytics that compare relative risk across instruments or scenarios in market-intelligence platforms.
items.example: An instrument with high volatility and thin liquidity receives a high risk score, with the contributing factors displayed alongside the value.
items.limitation: Analytical only; NOT financial or investment advice. A risk score is a heuristic characterisation, not a recommendation or guarantee. Past risk does not predict future outcomes.

items.title: Confidence bands
items.description: Confidence bands express the uncertainty around a forecast or estimate as a range rather than a single point, communicating that the future is not known precisely. They are derived from model error, input variability, or scenario spread and should widen as uncertainty grows. Presenting a band alongside any central estimate discourages over-interpretation of a single number. Software should always pair a forecast value with its uncertainty rather than displaying a bare point.
items.applicability: Applies to forecasting and estimation outputs across market-intelligence platforms.
items.example: A forecast is shown as a central value with an upper and lower band that widens further into the future, signalling growing uncertainty.
items.limitation: Analytical only; NOT financial or investment advice. A band conveys uncertainty, not a guarantee that outcomes fall within it. No recommendation implied; past performance does not predict future results.

items.title: Scenario forecasting
items.description: Scenario forecasting explores a range of plausible futures under different assumptions rather than asserting a single prediction. Each scenario states its assumptions and produces a conditional outcome, helping users understand sensitivity to key drivers. The approach makes uncertainty explicit and resists false precision. Systems should record the assumptions behind each scenario so results can be reproduced and challenged, and should never present a scenario as a definite forecast.
items.applicability: Applies to forward-looking analytics in market-intelligence platforms that model multiple possible outcomes.
items.example: Three scenarios, optimistic, central, and adverse, each state their assumptions and show a different conditional path, presented together to convey the range.
items.limitation: Analytical only; NOT financial or investment advice. Scenarios are illustrative conditional analyses, not predictions or recommendations. No outcome is guaranteed; past performance does not predict future results.

items.title: Backtesting discipline
items.description: Backtesting evaluates an analytical approach against historical data to estimate how it would have behaved, and must be done carefully to avoid misleading results. Discipline includes using only information available at each point in time, separating in-sample from out-of-sample data, and accounting for costs and realistic constraints. Without these safeguards, backtests flatter an approach and create false confidence. Systems should make the test methodology and data window explicit so results are honest and reproducible.
items.applicability: Applies to evaluating analytical models and strategies in market-intelligence platforms.
items.example: A backtest reserves the most recent period as out-of-sample, uses point-in-time data, and reports results after estimated costs rather than on an idealised basis.
items.limitation: Analytical only; NOT financial or investment advice. A backtest describes hypothetical past behaviour, not future results, and can mislead if flawed. Past performance does not predict future results.

items.title: Paper trading
items.description: Paper trading simulates decisions against live or recorded market data without committing real funds, to observe how an approach behaves going forward. It complements backtesting by testing on genuinely unseen data and surfacing practical issues such as timing and data delays. Results remain hypothetical because they exclude real execution effects like slippage and market impact. Systems should clearly label paper results as simulated and never present them as actual performance.
items.applicability: Applies to forward-testing and demonstration features in market-intelligence platforms.
items.example: A strategy runs in paper mode against live data for a quarter, with all positions and outcomes clearly labelled as simulated, not real.
items.limitation: Analytical only; NOT financial or investment advice. Paper results are hypothetical and exclude real execution effects; they do not represent actual returns. Past performance does not predict future results.

items.title: Watchlists and alerts
items.description: Watchlists group instruments a user wants to follow, and alerts notify when a defined analytical condition is met, such as a threshold crossing or an event of interest. Alerts are informational triggers to prompt review, not instructions to act. Good design lets users define conditions clearly, avoids alert fatigue through sensible defaults, and records why each alert fired. Systems should phrase alerts as observations rather than recommendations to keep their role clearly analytical.
items.applicability: Applies to monitoring and notification features in market-intelligence platforms.
items.example: A user adds an instrument to a watchlist and sets an alert for when a metric crosses a level; firing the alert notifies them to review, with the triggering condition shown.
items.limitation: Analytical only; NOT financial or investment advice. Alerts are informational triggers, not buy/sell instructions. No outcome is guaranteed; past behaviour does not predict future results.

items.title: Overfitting and look-ahead-bias warnings
items.description: Overfitting occurs when a model captures noise specific to historical data and fails on new data, while look-ahead bias occurs when an analysis inadvertently uses information that was not available at the decision time. Both produce results that appear strong but do not generalise, and both are common, easy-to-make errors. Guardrails include holding out data, enforcing point-in-time inputs, preferring simpler models, and validating on genuinely unseen periods. Systems should make these checks visible so users are warned when results may be unreliable.
items.applicability: Applies to model development and validation in market-intelligence and forecasting platforms.
items.example: A pipeline blocks features computed from future data and flags a model whose out-of-sample performance collapses, warning of likely overfitting.
items.limitation: Analytical only; NOT financial or investment advice. These are engineering safeguards, not assurances of accuracy. A model that passes them can still be wrong; past performance does not predict future results.

items.title: Disclaimers framework
items.description: A disclaimers framework ensures that analytical and forecasting outputs are consistently accompanied by clear statements of their nature and limits. It typically asserts that content is informational and not advice, that no outcome is guaranteed, and that past performance does not predict future results. Embedding disclaimers as a structural part of output, rather than optional text, keeps the platform's role honest and reduces the risk of misinterpretation. Disclaimer wording itself should be reviewed against applicable rules in each jurisdiction.
items.applicability: Applies platform-wide to any feature that presents market intelligence, signals, or forecasts to users.
items.example: Every forecast view renders a standard disclaimer stating the output is analytical, not advice, with no guaranteed outcome and no buy/sell recommendation.
items.limitation: Analytical only; NOT financial, investment, or legal advice. A disclaimer does not itself ensure regulatory compliance; financial-promotion and advice rules are jurisdiction-specific and must be confirmed against current applicable regulations. Past performance does not predict future results.
